questions.q: What is the primary difference between horizontal and vertical scaling?
options: Horizontal uses more memory; vertical uses more CPU
Horizontal adds more servers; vertical adds more resources (CPU, RAM) to existing servers
Horizontal is always cheaper; vertical has no upper limit
Horizontal is used only for databases; vertical is used only for applications
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling distributes load across multiple machines; vertical scaling upgrades a single machine's resources. Costs and limits vary for each approach. The other options are either reversed or factually incorrect.

questions.q: A stateful application session is stored in memory on Server A. If traffic increases and you add Server B with a load balancer routing new requests to it, what happens to existing user sessions?
options: Sessions automatically replicate to Server B
Sessions are lost and users must re-authenticate
The load balancer ensures all requests from a user go to Server A
Sessions are converted to cookies stored on the client
questions.explanation: Without session replication or a shared session store, Server B has no knowledge of sessions created on Server A. Users lose their sessions and must re-authenticate. Session stickiness (option 2) is a workaround, not a solution. Options 0 and 3 require additional infrastructure not described.

questions.q: Which caching strategy reduces the load on a primary database the most while minimizing stale data issues?
options: Write-through caching: write goes to cache first, then database synchronously
Write-behind caching: write goes to cache, database updated asynchronously
No caching to ensure all reads get fresh data
Cache all data permanently regardless of how often it changes
questions.explanation: Write-through ensures consistency by waiting for the database write before confirming success, so cache and database stay in sync. Write-behind improves write performance but risks data loss if the cache fails before database sync. The other options either eliminate caching or guarantee stale data.

questions.q: A web application experiences a sudden spike in traffic. The load balancer distributes requests evenly across servers, but response times degrade severely. Which bottleneck is most likely?
options: The load balancer itself is overloaded
The database is the bottleneck; application servers are underutilized
All servers have CPU at 100% simultaneously
Requests are too large and exceed network bandwidth
questions.explanation: Uneven resource utilization (servers idle while database saturates) indicates the database is the constraint. If application servers were the bottleneck, the load balancer would show high CPU. Options 0, 2, and 3 would manifest differently in monitoring.

questions.q: Which pattern helps prevent a single service from consuming all connection pool resources in a microservices system?
options: Rate limiting and backpressure
Caching on the client side
Using HTTP/1.0 instead of HTTP/2
Merging all services into one monolith
questions.explanation: Rate limiting restricts how many requests a service can make, and backpressure tells upstream services to slow down. This prevents resource exhaustion. Client-side caching doesn't manage server connections. HTTP versions and merging services don't solve connection pool exhaustion.

questions.q: In a database read-heavy workload, you implement read replicas. What consistency issue might arise?
options: Read replicas always crash under load
Replication lag: reads from replicas may return stale data if the primary hasn't finished writing
Read replicas violate ACID properties entirely
Queries to read replicas take longer than queries to the primary
questions.explanation: Asynchronous replication introduces a window where replicas lag behind the primary. Reads during this window see old data. Replicas don't inherently crash, don't violate ACID, and queries aren't inherently slower. The challenge is managing eventual consistency.

questions.q: Which stateless design principle is most important for horizontal scalability?
options: All requests must be read-only to prevent conflicts
No request should depend on data stored in previous requests to the same server
Every server must have identical hardware specifications
Databases must support distributed transactions across all nodes
questions.explanation: Statelessness allows any server to handle any request without knowledge of prior requests, enabling seamless load distribution. Option 0 prevents writes, which is impractical. Option 2 is helpful but not the core principle. Option 3 introduces complexity and contradicts scalability best practices.

questions.q: A batch processing system processes 1 million records sequentially, taking 10 hours. To reduce this to 1 hour, you partition data across 10 workers in parallel. Why might this still not achieve 10x speedup?
options: Parallel processing always adds 90% overhead, limiting speedup to 10%
Amdahl's Law: some parts (like I/O or serial bottlenecks) don't parallelize, limiting overall speedup below 10x
Modern CPUs can only run 10 operations per second maximum
Network latency makes parallel processing slower than sequential
questions.explanation: Amdahl's Law states that speedup is limited by non-parallelizable sections. If data partitioning, initialization, or final aggregation are serial, total speedup is less than 10x. The other options misunderstand parallel processing fundamentals.

questions.q: In a microservices architecture, a critical service experiences increased latency during peak hours. Adding more instances via horizontal scaling shows no improvement. What is the most likely cause?
options: The operating system cannot handle more than 5 instances per service
A shared external dependency (database, cache, API) is the bottleneck, not the service itself
The service is running 32-bit code instead of 64-bit code
Horizontal scaling only works for stateless services, and this service is stateful
questions.explanation: If adding capacity doesn't reduce latency, the service is not the bottleneck. A downstream dependency is likely saturated. Option 0 is false. Option 2 is unrelated. Option 3 may apply but statefulness alone isn't the issue—external dependencies are more common culprits.

questions.q: A system uses in-memory caching aggressively but still struggles with latency spikes. Which issue is most likely?
options: In-memory caching always fails under high concurrency
Cache misses on cold start or after cache evictions cause database thundering herd—many concurrent requests hit the database simultaneously
The system has too much memory and should reduce cache size
Caching violates REST principles and should be disabled
questions.explanation: Cache eviction or cold start causes a cascade of misses, and all requests simultaneously hit the database, creating a thundering herd. This causes latency spikes. Options 0, 2, and 3 are factually incorrect or don't explain the phenomenon.
